scheduled_catalysts: Micron fiscal Q3 earnings reaction after Wednesday after-bell release

market_session: regular US cash-equity session, fourth session after Juneteenth closure
text: The June 25, 2026 report date was a regular Thursday U.S. cash-equity and options session, the fourth session after the Juneteenth closure, so sentiment values can be read against a live U.S. closing tape and a full Cboe options settle. Two scheduled catalysts shaped the session: the 8:30 a.m. EDT May PCE inflation release and the cash-session reaction to Micron Technology's fiscal Q3 results, which had been reported after the bell on Wednesday June 24.

russell_2000.change_percent: +0.7% (+0.38% per TheStreet close update; +0.71% per AP rounded)

value.context: Mixed close: Micron-led memory rally vs Apple -6.13% and Microsoft -3.23% dragging mega-cap tech; Caterpillar +5.81% and UnitedHealth +2.65% lifting the Dow; rotation into cyclicals continued; VIX rose despite the easing in June 24's vol

text: AP reported a mixed June 25 U.S. close: the S&P 500 was essentially unchanged at 7,357.49 (-0.73 points, less than 0.1%) and the Nasdaq Composite fell 118.03 points or 0.5% to 25,358.60, its fourth straight decline, as Apple dropped about 6% after raising Mac and iPad prices and Microsoft fell 3.23% after announcing Xbox price increases. The Dow rose 71.72 points or 0.1% to 51,920.62 and the Russell 2000 gained 21.23 points or 0.7% to 3,007.86 on broader participation, with Caterpillar +5.81% and UnitedHealth +2.65% leading. The session's signal is a continuation of June 24's bifurcation: a Micron-led memory and chip rally versus persistent Magnificent 7 selling pressure, with the cap-weighted S&P 500 flat while the equal-weight and small-cap complex outperformed.

subject.name: CNN Fear & Greed Index
subject.source_basis: CNN Fear & Greed page corroborated by Finhacker.cz CNN-tracker

value.methodology_note: CNN Stock Price Breadth indicator still reflects the McClellan Summation Index methodology change noted in prior reports
value.freshness_note: CNN's 26 is an intraday June 25 snapshot (1:59 PM ET); Finhacker's 26 reflects the June 24 post-close value. Both publishers now agree on 26, unlike June 24 when CNN read 27 and Finhacker 28.

market_session: latest available readings at collection time
text: The Fear & Greed Index remained in fear territory on June 25. CNN's page displayed 26 (fear) with 'Previous close 25', '1 week ago 37', '1 month ago 58', '1 year ago 59', and 'Last updated Jun 25 at 1:59:33 PM ET'. Finhacker.cz's CNN-tracker also showed 26 (fear) with a data-updated timestamp of 'Jun 24, 2026, 8:00 PM ET'. Unlike June 24, when CNN and Finhacker disagreed by one point (27 vs 28), both publishers now converge on 26. Five of the seven CNN sub-indicators were labeled fear or extreme fear at the time of access: market momentum (fear), stock price strength (fear), stock price breadth (extreme fear, McClellan Volume Summation Index at 1,400.47), put/call options (fear, 5-day average 0.70), and market volatility (neutral, with VIX below its 50-day moving average). The composite moved up one point from the June 24 close of 25 but stayed firmly in the fear zone, down 11 points from one month ago.

nine_day_thirty_day_spread.note: VIX9D-to-VIX gap widened; the 9-day measure eased while the 30-day measure rose

market_session: Cboe published volatility-index data for the regular US session

calculation.method: VIX one-day percent change = (18.89 - 18.63) / 18.63 * 100 = +1.40%; VIX9D one-day change = (17.92 - 18.07) / 18.07 * 100 = -0.83%; curve compares Cboe close fields for VIX9D, VIX, VIX3M, VIX6M, and VIX1Y on 2026-06-25

text: Yahoo Finance historical data showed VIX closing at 18.89 on June 25, up 0.26 points or 1.40% from 18.63 on June 24, with OHLC of open 18.11, high 19.95, low 17.72, close 18.89. Investing.com corroborates at 18.90 (+1.45%) with a 4:14:46 PM close stamp. The term structure was in contango and upward sloping across all tenors: VIX9D at 17.92, VIX at 18.89, VIX3M at 20.33, VIX6M at 22.35, and VIX1Y at 23.50. The notable feature is a divergence between the 9-day and the 30-day measures: VIX9D eased 0.83% to 17.92 (the near-term event risk of the PCE morning passing without a tape break was priced out), while VIX rose 1.40% to 18.89 (the cash-session mega-cap tech selloff and the Strait of Hormuz strike repriced the 30-day window). The VIX9D-to-VIX gap widened to 0.97 points from June 24's 0.56-point spread. The longer tenors all eased slightly (VIX3M -0.20%, VIX6M -0.22%, VIX1Y -1.48%), keeping the curve in orderly contango. Front-month July VIX futures settled June 24 at 18.90, a +0.27-point premium to spot that signals mild contango rather than acute stress.

market_session: latest AAII weekly survey published for the June 25 report date

calculation.method: Bull-bear spread = bullish 44.9% minus bearish 36.1% = +8.8 percentage points; prior spread = 36.6 - 39.4 = -2.8 pp; one-week swing = 8.8 - (-2.8) = +11.6 pp

text: The latest AAII sentiment survey (week ending 6/24/2026, published Thursday June 25) showed a sharp bullish leap: bullish sentiment at 44.9%, neutral at 18.9%, and bearish at 36.1%, leaving a bull-bear spread of +8.8 percentage points. This is the first time bullish sentiment has been above its historical average of 37.5% in six weeks. The swing from the prior week's -2.8 pp spread is an 11.6-percentage-point one-week jump, the largest bullish shift since the run-up to the January 2026 highs. The AAII companion article was titled 'Optimism Leaps.' The survey period closed before the June 25 cash-session action, so the bullish print does not reflect the fourth-straight Nasdaq decline; it captures the improvement in mood driven by the Iran de-escalation, the Micron-guidance-led chip rally, and the in-line May PCE release. The special question asked about the US-Iran memorandum of understanding's impact on economic expectations.

spx_put_call_ratio.source: Barchart S&P 500 Index Put/Call for Thu Jun 25 2026

value.interpretation: Two-tier pattern: single-stock options lean bullish (more calls than puts) while index and ETF flow leans defensive (more puts than calls), consistent with institutional tail-hedging alongside retail bullishness in single names

market_session: Cboe end-of-day options statistics for the regular US session
text: Cboe end-of-day options statistics for June 25 showed a two-tier put/call pattern. The total put/call ratio was 1.02, with equity put/call at 0.61, index put/call at 1.16, and exchange-traded products put/call at 1.38. Barchart's S&P 500 index put/call for June 25 showed put volume of 907,215 against call volume of 694,491, a ratio of 1.31. The pattern - bullish single-stock options flow (equity P/C 0.61, well below 1.0) alongside defensive index and ETF hedging (index P/C 1.16, ETP P/C 1.38, SPX P/C 1.31) - is consistent with institutions buying tail protection via index products while retail positioning remains bullish on individual names, particularly the Micron-led memory and chip complex. The CNN Fear & Greed 5-day average put/call ratio of 0.70 (fear zone) is a different construction from the Cboe end-of-day ratios but points in the same direction: options-market sentiment is cautious overall, with the defensive bid concentrated in index products.

value.data_freshness_note: ICI's latest combined-flow release (published June 24 for the week ended June 17) predates the June 23-24 risk-off action and the June 25 PCE/Micron session; the next ICI release (week ended June 24) had not been published at collection time. ETF.com's June 22 weekly article captures the post-SpaceX-IPO euphoria week but also predates the late-week tech selloff. Positioning context, not real-time flow.

market_session: latest fund-flow releases available for the June 25 report date
text: The latest available fund-flow data provides positioning context heading into the June 25 session rather than a real-time flow gauge. ICI's combined-flow release (published June 24 for the week ended June 17) showed $66.23 billion of total long-term inflows, including $92.03 billion of ETF net issuance and $25.80 billion of mutual fund outflows. Equity funds took in $55.75 billion (domestic $49.60B, world $6.15B), bond funds took in $16.46 billion, hybrid funds saw $4.68 billion in outflows, and commodity funds saw $1.31 billion in outflows. ETF.com's June 22 weekly article reported $119.83 billion of total ETF inflows led by $83.62 billion into U.S. equity ETFs and $21.66 billion into international equity ETFs, with IVV (+$30.40B), SPYM (+$12.87B), XLK (+$8.33B), and SMH (+$7.44B) the top creators and SPY (-$29.62B) and VOO (-$14.42B) the top redemptions. The flows point to strong risk appetite into the late-week selloff but do not capture the June 23-25 tape action; the SPY/VOO redemptions alongside IVV/SPYM creations suggest structural rotation (share-class creation flow) rather than outright equity abandonment.

value.brent_note: reversal from pre-war drift on Strait of Hormuz vessel strike

may_headline_pce.note: three-year high, in line with forecasts

value.macro_theme: Iran de-escalation vs Hormuz flare-up, PCE at three-year high but in line, Micron-led risk-on in chips, mega-cap tech drag

text: Reuters reported Brent crude settled up 2% at $75.26 a barrel, reversing several days of declines toward pre-US-Iran-war levels on reports of an Iranian strike on a vessel in the Strait of Hormuz. The dollar index eased 0.15% to 101.45 from Wednesday's one-year high. Gold recovered from a sub-$4,000 print to around $4,045. The 10-year Treasury yield slipped 0.59 bp to 4.394%, its lowest since mid-May, as the May PCE report came in at a three-year-high 4.1% year-over-year headline (3.4% core) but matched forecasts. The macro backdrop is contradictory for sentiment: the in-line PCE print and easing yields are risk-positive, the Hormuz strike and the 4%+ inflation are risk-negative, and the cross-asset price action - Brent up, dollar down, gold up, yields down - is a classic geopolitical-risk bid rather than a clean risk-on or risk-off tape. Against this backdrop, the AAII survey's jump to net bullish reflects the Iran de-escalation narrative and the chip rally more than it prices the lingering mega-cap tech overhang.

subject.label: rotational risk-on with institutional hedging, mega-cap tech still bleeding

value.cash_equity_tape: mixed close with S&P flat, Nasdaq -0.5% (4th straight), Dow +0.1%, Russell +0.7%; Caterpillar +5.81%, UnitedHealth +2.65%, Micron +15% offset Apple -6.13%, Microsoft -3.23%
value.survey_sentiment: sharp AAII bullish leap to 44.9% / +8.8 pp spread, first above-average in 6 weeks
value.volatility: VIX +1.40% to 18.89; VIX9D -0.83% to 17.92; contango curve with widening 9D-30D gap
value.options: two-tier: equity P/C 0.61 (bullish single names), index P/C 1.16 and ETP P/C 1.38 (defensive hedging)
value.flows: latest available flows are pre-late-week-selloff; $83B into US equity ETFs in the SpaceX-IPO week
value.fear_greed: CNN and Finhacker converge at 26 (fear), down from 37 a week ago
value.risk_read: sentiment is off the June 23 panic lows but not risk-on; the bullish narrative is driven by chips and Iran de-escalation, while mega-cap tech weakness and PCE above 4% cap the upside

market_session: regular US cash-equity session synthesis from latest available sentiment indicators
text: The best synthesis for June 25 is 'rotational risk-on under tail hedge, mega-cap tech still bleeding.' The AAII survey's 11.6-percentage-point one-week swing to a +8.8 pp bull-bear spread is the clearest sentiment signal of the cycle, and it aligns with the cash-session action: money flowed into the Micron-led memory and chip complex (+15% on Micron, +22% on Sandisk), into cyclicals (Caterpillar +5.81%), and into small caps (Russell +0.7%), while the Magnificent 7 continued to bleed (Apple -6.13%, Microsoft -3.23%) and the Nasdaq booked a fourth straight decline. The Fear & Greed Index held at 26 (fear) with CNN and Finhacker now converging, and the CNN sub-indicators showed extreme fear in stock price breadth (McClellan Volume Summation Index at 1,400.47) despite the neutral reading on market volatility. The VIX term structure captured the cross-currents perfectly: VIX9D eased 0.83% as the PCE event cleared, while VIX rose 1.40% as the cash-session tech selloff and the Hormuz strike repriced the 30-day window, widening the 9D-to-30D gap to 0.97 points. The Cboe put/call pattern - equity 0.61, index 1.16, ETP 1.38 - confirms that institutions are paying up for tail protection via index products while staying long the single-stock stories they like. The regime is therefore not the June 23 unambiguous risk-off and not a clean risk-on: it is a rotational, narrative-driven risk appetite (chips, Iran de-escalation, in-line PCE) layered over a persistent mega-cap tech overhang and an unresolved above-4% inflation backdrop, with institutional hedging visibly bid.
